content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 24, 2009

Catherine L. DiCamillo
City Attorney
City of South Lake Tahoe
1901 Airport Road, Ste. 300
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150-7051

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-028

Dear Ms. DiCamillo:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of South Lake Tahoe Councilmember Hal Cole regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)

Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Cole make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions regarding general resolutions relating to a redevelopment project despite owning multiple parcels of real property within the City of South Lake Tahoe limits including three residential rental properties within 500 feet of the boundaries of the project area?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Cole's economic interests in real properties within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the redevelopment project are directly involved in the governmental
decisions regarding the general resolutions you have described. The financial effect of
these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Councilmember Cole may not
make, participate in making, or influence the decisions unless he can rebut the
presumption by showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable the governmental decision
will have any reasonably material financial effects on his real property, and he also determines that there will
be no reasonably material financial effects on his other economic interests.
The "public generally" exception does not apply to Councilmember Cole's properties.

FACTS

The City of South Lake Tahoe formed the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment
Agency (the "RDA") in 1988. The RDA is composed of the five South Lake Tahoe City
Council Members including Councilmember Cole. Councilmember Cole owns eleven
parcels of real property within the city. Three of these eleven parcels are residential
rental properties located near a proposed redevelopment area known as the South Lake
Tahoe Redevelopment Area #2 ("Area #2").

It is anticipated that several items relating to Area #2 will soon be coming before
the city council and the RDA including the following:

•

A resolution of the RDA approving the exclusion of land from the proposed
boundaries of Area #2. (This resolution may remove certain land from Area #2,
involves only nonresidential properties, and will not affect the number of
residential parcels or residential property owners in the project area.)

•

A resolution of the RDA approving the preliminary report for Area #2 and rules
governing participation and preferences by owners, operators of businesses, and
tenants; consenting to a joint public hearing with the city council on the proposed
redevelopment plan for Area #2; authorizing transmittal of documents; and other
actions relating to the plan.

•

A resolution of the city council authorizing and consenting to a joint public
hearing with the RDA on the proposed redevelopment plan for Area #2.

As proposed, Area #2 spans approximately half of the length of the city.
Councilmember Cole's three parcels, near the project, include two side-by-side parcels
that are immediately adjacent to Area #2 and a third parcel within 500 feet of Area #2. It
is anticipated that these three parcels will remain within 500 feet of Area #2 even if the
RDA passes the planned resolution removing certain nonresidential areas from the
project area.

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 3

In terms of the overall project:

• The number of residential parcels² within city limits is 12,088. Including
timeshares, the number of residential parcels within city limits is 13,663.

• The number of residential parcels in the project area is 1,667. There are no
timeshares within project area.

• The number of residential parcels outside the project area but directly adjacent to
the boundaries of the project is 115. There are no timeshares adjacent to the
boundaries of the project.

• The number of residential parcels outside the project but within 500 feet of the
boundaries is 2,311. Including timeshares, the number of residential parcels
outside the project area but within 500 feet of the boundaries is 2,464.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Is Councilmember Cole a "public official"?

The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the South Lake Tahoe City Council,
Councilmember Cole is a public official within the meaning of the Act.³

________________________________

² As used, "residential parcels" include single-family homes, multi-family structures of four units
or fewer on a single lot, and condominium units. (See Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B)(ii).)

³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 files" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for
the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 4


Step Two: Is Councilmember Cole making, participating in making, or influencing
a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Councilmember Cole is making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision when acting in his role as a city council member.

Step Three: What are Councilmember Cole's "economic interests?"

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);

speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 5

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5).

From your account of the facts the Councilmember Cole appears to have the
following economic interests:

Real Property:

You have stated that Councilmember Cole owns eleven parcels of real property
within the City of South Lake Tahoe including three parcels within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the project area. From all indications it appears that Councilmember
Cole's investment in each of these eleven parcels is $2,000 or more. Accordingly,
Councilmember Cole has an economic interest in each of these real properties. (Section
87103(b).)

Rental Business:

You have indicated that at least three of Councilmember Cole's eleven parcels are
residential rental properties. Presumably, Councilmember Cole has an investment of
$2,000 or more in his rental business and will receive income of $500 or more in the 12
months prior to the decision from this business. Therefore, Councilmember Cole has an
economic interest in his rental business as both a business entity and a source of income.
(Section 87103 (a), (c), and (d).)

Tenants:

Provided that Councilmember Cole receives income of $500 or more in the 12
months prior to the decision from any particular tenant of one of his rental units,
Councilmember Cole has an economic interest in the tenant as a source of income.
(Section 87103(c).)

Personal Finances:

Councilmember Cole will always have an economic interest in his personal
finances and those of his immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect
on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 6

Step Four: Are Councilmember Cole's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the decision?

Real Property:

Regulation 18704.2 (copy enclosed) lists the factors that determine whether an
economic interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. Subdivision (a)(1) states a general rule that when a public official's real
property is within 500 feet from the boundaries of a proposed project, that real property is
directly involved in decisions relative to that project. In addition, subdivision (a)(1) and
(a)(5) states that when a public official's real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries of a redevelopment area, that real property is directly involved in any
governmental decision to "designate the survey area, to select the project area, to adopt
the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the environmental
document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or
to rescind or amend any of [these] decisions."4 Because Councilmember Cole's three
residential rental units are within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision,
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in these properties are directly involved in the
decisions regarding the resolutions you have described.5

Rental Business and Tenants:

Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved.

4 From the facts submitted, it does not appear that the other factors of Regulation 18704.2 are
applicable to Councilmember Cole's situation.

5 We note that you have not provided any information regarding the additional eight parcels of
property owned by Councilmember Cole within the City of South Lake Tahoe. In light of our analysis of
Councilmember Cole's three residential rental properties within 500 feet of Area #2, we do not find it
necessary to further consider the additional eight parcels.

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 7

(Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.1(b).) From the facts provided, it does not appear that
Councilmember Cole's rental business or tenants of this rental business have initiated the
proceeding, or are a party in, or subject of, the proceeding within the meaning of
Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it appears as though Councilmember Cole's economic
interests in his rental business and any tenants of this business are only indirectly
involved in decisions regarding the general resolutions you have described.

Personal Finances:

As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
financial effect on Council Cole's personal finances, Councilmember Cole's economic
interest in his personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Cole's economic interest(s)?

Materiality

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.

Real Property:

Any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (Ibid.)
However, the size of the financial effect does not matter. If there is any financial effect at
all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."

Rental Business:

For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c).

The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. While we do not know the size of Councilmember Cole's business, Regulation
18705.1(c)(4) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business,
not publicly traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 8

foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."

However, Councilmember Cole should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure
that he identifies the materiality threshold actually appropriate to his rental business.

Tenants:

When a source of income to a public official is an individual who is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision, the applicable materiality standard is found at
Regulation 18705.3(b)(3):

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other
than real property) by $1,000 or more; or

(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under … [Regulation] 18705.2(b)."

Personal Finances:

Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation
18705.5(a), which provides:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period.
When determining whether a governmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 9


Under Regulation 18705.5(a), neither a financial effect on the value of real
property Councilmember Cole owns, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues,
expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of his rental business would be considered a
material financial effect on his economic interest in his personal finances. However, any
other governmental decision which affects Councilmember Cole's personal finances by
$250 or more in any 12-month period is considered a material financial effect.

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.

We note that you have not submitted any facts indicating a financial effect on
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in his tenants as sources of income or his
personal finances. Accordingly, we cannot analyze these economic interests further.

You have, however, indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in his real property and his rental business.
While the determination of whether the financial effect is material must ultimately be left
up to Councilmember Cole's informed judgment, the financial effects on Councilmember
Cole's economic interests in his three residential rental properties are presumed to be
material because the properties are within 500 feet of the property subject to the
governmental decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Cole may not make, participate in
making, or influence the decisions regarding the resolutions you have described, unless
he can prove the presumption has been rebutted and determine that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on his other economic interests, such as
his rental business, tenants to his rental business, or his personal finances.

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 10


Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Regulation 18707.1(a) establishes a two-pronged
test to determine if the material financial effect of a governmental decision on a public
official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
Under this test, the governmental decision must affect a "significant segment" of the
public generally and must financially affect the public official's economic interest in
"substantially the same manner" as it affects the significant segment identified.
(Regulation 18707.1 (b)(1) and (b)(2).)

For economic interests in real properties,6 Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B) provides that
a governmental decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the
decision also affects ten percent or more of all property owners or all residential property
owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency.

The second prong of the public generally test requires that the decision affects the
public official's economic interest in "substantially the same manner" as the significant
segment identified. However, Councilmember Coles is the owner of eleven parcels of real
property within the City of South Lake Tahoe including the three residential rental
properties within Oaks Pet Area #2. Accordingly, even if the decisions regarding the
resolutions affect a significant segment, it is unlikely that Councilmember Cole's economic
interests in real properties will be affected in "substantially the same manner" as the
significant segment. For example, in the Shirey Advice Letter, No. A-06-132, we stated that
"given the fact that there are two properties owned by the Commissioner, it is unlikely that
the dollar value impact on these two properties will be substantially the same as on property
owners' homes where they only own one home."

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

___________________________
6 We have limited our analysis of the public generally exception as it applies to Councilmember
Cole's economic interests in real properties. Notwithstanding an analysis of Councilmember Cole's
economic interests in real properties, we note that Councilmember Cole must still determine whether
any particular decision will affect his economic interests in his rental business, tenants of the rental
business, or his personal finances prior to making, participating in making, or influencing the decision.

File No. A-09-028
Page No. 11


You have not presented any facts indicating that the "legally required
participation" exception is applicable to Councilmember Cole's circumstances. Thus, we
do not address this exception.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel


By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

Cole's investment in each of these eleven parcels is $Z,OOO or more. Accordingly,
Councilmember Cole has an economic interest in each of these real properties. (Section
87103(b).)
Rental Business:
You have indicated that at least three of Councilmember Cole's eleven parcels are
residential rental properties. Presumably, Councilmember Cole has an investment of
$2,000 or more in his rental business and will receive income of $500 or more in the 12
months prior to the decision from this business. Therefore, Councilmember Cole has an
economic interest in his rental business as both a business entity and a source of income.
(Section 87103 (a), (c), and (d).)
Tenants:
Provided that Councilmember Cole receives income of $500 or more inthe 12
months prior to the decision from any particular tenant of one of his rental units,
Councilmember Cole has an economic interest in the tenant as a source of income.
(Section 87103(c).)
Personal Finances:
Councilmember Cole will always have an economic interest in his personal
finances and those of his immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect
on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate iamily increasing or decreasing.
(Section 87103; Regularion 18703.5.)
Step Four: Are Councilmember Cole's economic interests direcfly or indirectly
involved in the decision?
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2 (copy enclosed) lists the factors that determine whether an
economic interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. Subdivision (a)(l) states a general rule that *hen a public offIcial,s real
property is within 500 feet from the boundaries of a proposed project, that real property is
directly involved in decisions relative to that project. kr addition, subdivision tixi) ana
(a)(5) states that when a public official's real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries of a redevelopment area, that real property is directly involved in any
governmental decision to "designate the survey area, to select the project area, to adopt
the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the enuironmental
document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to tirr redevelopment area, or
to rescind or amend any of [these] decisions."o Because Councilmember Ctle's three
residential rental units are within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision,
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in these prop"rti"r are directly involved in the
decisions regarding the resolutions you have describedJ
Rental Business and Tenants:
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when ihat business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the officiai,s ug"r.ylA
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves tt " irruun"",
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarde d as indirecrly involved.
a From the facts submitted, it does not appear that the other factors of Regulation lg:/04.2 are
applicable to Councilmember Cole's situation.
5 We note that you have not provided any infbrmation regarding the additional eight parcels of
property owned by Councilmember Cole within the City of South Lake Tahoe. In light of iur analysis of
Councilmember Cole's three residential rental properties within 500 t'eet of Area #2, we do not find it
necessary to further consider the additional eight parcels.
(Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.1(b).) From the facts provided, it does not appear that
Councilmember Cole's rental business or tenants of this rental business have initiated the
proceeding, or are a party in, or subject of, the proceeding within the meaning of
Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it appears as though Councilmember Cole's economic
interests in his rental business and any tenants of this business are only indirectly
involved in decisions regarding the general resolutions you have desciibed.
Personal Finances:
As for your economic interest in your personal finances, if facts suggest any
financial effect on Council Cole's personal finances, Councilmember Coleli economic
interest in his personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Cole's econornic interest(s)?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Real Property:
Any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. lRegulation iglOS.Zia)(t).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
govenrmental decision will have any financial effect on the real prbperty. (lbid.)
However, the size of the financial effect does not matter. If theri is aryfinancial effect at
all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."
Rental Business:
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. While we do not know the size of Councilmember iole's business, Regulation
18705.1(c)(4) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a business,
not publicly traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
However, Councilmember Cole should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure
that he identifies the materiality threshold actually appropiiute to his rental business.
Tenants:
When a source of income to a public official is an individual who is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision, the applicable materiality standard is found at
Regulation I 8705.3(bX3):
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income,
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other
than real property) by $1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under ... [Regulation] 1g705-.2(b).,, 
-
Personal Finances:
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation
I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official,s
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period.
when determining whether a governmentar decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effeci on ttre
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."


File No. ,{-09-028
Under Regulation 18705.5(a), neither a financial effect on the value of real
property Councilmember Cole owns, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues,
expenses' or value of assets and liabilities of his rental business would be considered a
material financial effect on his economic interest in his personal finances. However, any
other govemmental decision which affects Councilmember Cole's personal finances by
$250 or more in any l2-month period is considered a material financial effect.
Foreseeability
- 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is ..reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseea'ble,,'but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particutai financiil effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to it . infb.-.d judgment of the public
official.
We note that you have not submitted any facts indicating a financial effect on
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in his tenants as sources of income or his
personal finances. Accordingly, we cannot analyzethese economic interests further.
You have, however, indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
Councilmember Cole's economic interests in his real property and his rental business.
While the determination of whether the financial effeCt is material must ultimately be left
up to Councilmember Cole's informed judgment, the financial effects on Councilmember
Cole's economic interests in his three residential rental properties are presumed to be
material because the properties are within 500 feet of the pioperty subject to the
govemmental decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Cole may not make, participate in
making, or influence the decisions regarding the resolutions you huue described, unless
he can prove the presumption has been rebutted and determine that there willbe no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on his other economic interests, such as
his rental business, tenants to his rental business, or his personal finances.
Steps Seven and Eighft Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Regulation 18707.1(a) establishls u t*-o-prorgld
test to determine if the material financial effect of a govemmental decision on a public
official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public genirally.
Under this test, the governmental decision must affect a "significant slgment-" of the
public generally and must financially affect the public official's ""ororrri" interest in
"substantially the same manner" as it affects the significant segment identified.
(Regulation 18707 .1 (bXl) and (b)(2).)
For economic interests in real properties,6 Regulation 18707.1(b)(l)(B) provides that
a govemmental decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public g"neruily if the
decision also affects ten percent or more of all property owners o. ilt residential p.op"rty
owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency.
The second prong of the public generally test requires that the decision affects the
public official's economic interest in "substantially the same manner" as the significant
segment identified. However, Councilmember Coles is the owner of eleven picels of real
property within the City of South Lake Tahoe including the three residential iental
properties within 500 feet of Area #2. Accordingly, even if the decisions regarding the
resolutions affect a significant segment, it is unlikely that Councilmember C-ole,s economic
interests in real properties will be affected in "substantially the same manner,' as the
significant segment. For example, in the Shirey Advice Letter, No. 4-06-132,we stated that
"given the fact that there are two properties owned by the Commissioner, it is unlikely that
the dollar value impacton these two properties will be substantially the same as on property
owners' homes where they only own one home."
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the officiai may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exciption aiplies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the governmint agency would Ue paratyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
6 we have limited our analysis of the public generally exception as it applies to Councilmember
Cole's economic interests in real properties. Notwithstanding an anaiysis of Councilmember Cole's
economic interests in the real properties, we note tfrat Councilmembei Cole must still determine whether
any particular decision will atfect his economic interests in his rental business, tenants of the rental
business, or his personal finances prior to making, participating in making, or influencing the decision.


File No. ,{-09-028
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "legally required
participation" exception is applicable to Councilmember Cole's circumsiances. Thus, we
do not address this exception.

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Cole's economic interests in real properties within 500 feet of the
boundaries of the redevelopment project are directly invotved in the govemmental
decisions regarding the general resolutions you havl described. The-financial effect of
these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Councilmember Cole may not
make, participate in making, or influence the decisions Lnless he can rebut the
presumption by showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable the govemmental decision
will have any financial effect on his real property, an6 he also determines that there will
\? no reasonably foreseeable material financial effe"ts on his other economic interests.
The "public generally" exception does not apply to Councilmember Cole's properties.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 24, 2009 Catherine L. DiCamillo City Attorney City of South Lake Tahoe 1901 Airport Road, Ste. 300 South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150-7051 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-09-028 Dear Ms. DiCamillo: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of South Lake Tahoe Councilmember Hal Cole regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION May Councilmember Cole make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions regarding general resolutions relating to a redevelopment project despite owning multiple parcels of real property within the City of South Lake Tahoe limits including three residential rental properties within 500 feet of the boundaries of the project area? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-09-028 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Councilmember Cole's economic interests in real properties within 500 feet of the boundaries of the redevelopment project are directly involved in the governmental decisions regarding the general resolutions you have described. The financial effect of these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Councilmember Cole may not make, participate in making, or influence the decisions unless he can rebut the presumption by showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable the governmental decision will have any reasonably material financial effects on his real property, and he also determines that there will be no reasonably material financial effects on his other economic interests. The "public generally" exception does not apply to Councilmember Cole's properties. FACTS The City of South Lake Tahoe formed the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Agency (the "RDA") in 1988. The RDA is composed of the five South Lake Tahoe City Council Members including Councilmember Cole. Councilmember Cole owns eleven parcels of real property within the city. Three of these eleven parcels are residential rental properties located near a proposed redevelopment area known as the South Lake Tahoe Redevelopment Area #2 ("Area #2"). It
